coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemical aptasensor based on PANI/GQDs for the detection of profenofos residues in vegetables
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yinghui
He, Zhenying
Fang, Mingxuan
Huang, Jingcheng
Zhao, Jicheng
Guo, Yemin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an electrochemical aptasensor was constructed based on a conductive polymer polyaniline (PANI) doped with Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) nanocomposites. The aptasensor quantitatively detected profenofos pesticide residues in vegetables by measuring characteristic peak signals using the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. We successfully prepared N-GQDs by one-step hydrothermal method and PANI/N-GQDs by in-situ polymerization. The abundant functional groups presented in PANI significantly enhanced adhesion of N-GQDs, thereby improving electrochemical activity of composite material. N-GQDs had a large number of N-containing functional groups, which could be used as electron transfer agents to improve sensitivity of sensor. N-GQDs and PANI/N-GQDs were comprehensively characterized using microscopic characterization tools. Under optimal experimental conditions, the aptasensor demonstrated excellent linearity for profenofos pesticide within a concentration range of 1 × 10−1 ng/mL to 1 × 104 ng/mL, with a correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9953). The limit of detection was determined to be 3.59 ng/mL, and recovery rates for profenofos in vegetables varied from 96.69 % to 101.71 %. Thus, this simple and ultrasensitive assay provides a new and promising tool for profenofos residues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical
Polyaniline
Graphene quantum dots
Profenofos
Aptasensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PANI/N-GQDs composites (in-situ polymerization method)
Optimization of experimental conditions for sensors
Detection of the pesticide profenofos by aptasensors
Specificity, stability and reproducibility of aptasensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B, C) TEM images of N-GQDs, with inset histograms of particle size distribution of N-GQDs; (D) Fourier-variant infrared spectra of N-GQDs.
(A) (B): TEM images of PANI/N-GQDs; (C) (D): SEM images of PANI/N-GQDs.
(A) EDS electronic image of PANI/N-GQDs; (B) EDS layered image of PANI/N-GQDs; (C) Total EDS spectrum of PANI/N-GQDs; (D) (E) (F).
(A) XPS survey spectrum of PANI/N-GQDs; (B) C1s XPS fine spectrum of PANI/N-GQDs; (C) O1s XPS fine spectrum of PANI/N-GQDs; (D) N1s XPS fine spectrum of PANI/N-GQDs.
(A) CV characterization of the aptasensor assembly process; (B) EIS characterization of the aptasensor assembly process.
(A) Optimization of PANI/N-GQDs concentration; (B) Optimization of aptamer concentration; (C) Optimization of pesticide incubation time; (D) Optimization of electrolyte pH.
(A) DPV response of the aptasensor to different concentrations of profenofos; (B) linear relationship between peak DPV current and logarithm of profenofos concentration.

(A) Sensor specificity: a. Malathion, b. Methomyl, c. Azinphos-methyl, d. Chlorpyrifos, e. profenofos, f. profenofos and other pesticide mixtures; (B) Stability test of sensors; (C) Repeatability test of sensors.
Schematic illustration of the aptasensor assembled procedure.
Actual sample testing spiked recovery test results.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemical aptasensor based on PANI/GQDs for the detection of profenofos residues in vegetables

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo, Shandong 255049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo, Shandong 255049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Engineering Research Center of Vegetable Safety and Quality Traceability, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo, Shandong 255049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Engineering Research Center of Vegetable Safety and Quality Traceability

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Engineering Research Center of Vegetable Safety and Quality Traceability, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo, Shandong 255049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zibo City Key Laboratory of Agricultural Product Safety Traceability, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo, Shandong 255049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zibo City Key Laboratory of Agricultural Product Safety Traceability

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zibo City Key Laboratory of Agricultural Product Safety Traceability, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo, Shandong 255049, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo, Shandong 255049, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an electrochemical aptasensor was constructed based on a conductive polymer polyaniline (PANI) doped with Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) nanocomposites. The aptasensor quantitatively detected profenofos pesticide residues in vegetables by measuring characteristic peak signals using the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. We successfully prepared N-GQDs by one-step hydrothermal method and PANI/N-GQDs by in-situ polymerization. The abundant functional groups presented in PANI significantly enhanced adhesion of N-GQDs, thereby improving electrochemical activity of composite material. N-GQDs had a large number of N-containing functional groups, which could be used as electron transfer agents to improve sensitivity of sensor. N-GQDs and PANI/N-GQDs were comprehensively characterized using microscopic characterization tools. Under optimal experimental conditions, the aptasensor demonstrated excellent linearity for profenofos pesticide within a concentration range of 1 × 10−1 ng/mL to 1 × 104 ng/mL, with a correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9953). The limit of detection was determined to be 3.59 ng/mL, and recovery rates for profenofos in vegetables varied from 96.69 % to 101.71 %. Thus, this simple and ultrasensitive assay provides a new and promising tool for profenofos residues.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data for this work will be made available upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Profenofos is a widely utilized broad-spectrum organophosphorus pesticide [1]. It is primarily employed to control various chewing and sucking pests, as well as mites affecting fruits, vegetables, tea plants, cotton and other agricultural crops [2,3]. Profenofos also a detrimental insecticide to humans, entering the body through contaminated food or skin infections [4,5] and inhibiting activity of cholinesterase, causing it to lose ability to decompose acetylcholine [6]. This inhibition results in an accumulation of acetylcholine, thereby disrupting normal cholinergic nerve function within the central nervous system [7] and potentially causing respiratory distress [8]. Prolonged or excessive exposure to profenofos may elicit severe toxic reactions that jeopardizes human health [9,10], manifesting symptoms such as dizziness, nausea, vomiting, and, death in extreme cases [11]. Furthermore, improper handling practices may lead to environmental contamination [12,13].

In recent years, various methods for detecting profenofos residues have been investigated, encompassing liquid chromatography (LC) [14–16], gas chromatography (GC) [17–19], and mass spectrometry (MS) [20–22]. These methods are precise, and they entail long test durations and high costs, demanding specialized operators [23]. Electrochemical sensor has garnered significant attention from scholars on account of its high sensitivity, rapid response time, favorable stability and linear range, low power consumption, repeatability, low cost, etc. It has emerged as a preferred choice for detecting bioactive components and has become a research focus in the field of biochemical analysis [24–27].

Polyaniline (PANI) is a conductive polymer compound characterized by the single and double bond structures. It has been widely used in the field of sensing due to its excellent spatial structure, flexibility and biocompatibility [28,29]. However, sensor built with PANI has limited detection range and poor catalytic activity in neutral electrolytes, its stability is problematic, and it is susceptible to oxidative degradation and corrosion under air and light conditions [30]. To address these challenges, it is essential to incorporate carbon-based material composites known for their exceptional conductivity, high chemical stability, and large surface area. Examples of such materials include carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and reduced graphene oxide [24,31].

GQDs are zero-dimensional graphene particles with a size of less than 20 nm. Owing to their exceptional electrical conductivity, high specific surface area and favorable optical properties [32,33], they have become one of the most promising materials for the future development of biosensors. Currently, researchers are intrigued by doping GQDs with heteroatoms such as N, P, B, F and S [34]. The doping of heteroatoms improves electrical conductivity of composites, due to high electronegativity difference between C and heteroatoms results in electronic network disorientation and charge redistribution [35,36]. The exceptional electron transfer capability and tunable electronic properties of GQDs can further enhance electrical conductivity of composites [37].

In this work, an ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor was developed, utilizing PANI/N-GQDs nanocomposites as the substrate. AuNPs were utilized as signal-amplifying elements, and an aptamer (Apt) served as the recognition element, enabling the detection of profenofos residues in vegetables [38]. As an electrically conductive polymer with a high specific surface area [39], PANI could act as a matrix for better attach N-GQDs. N-GQDs provided an abundance of N-functional groups as electron transfer agents, enhancing overall electroactivity of composite material [40]. N-GQDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method [41]. PANI/N-GQDs were prepared through an in-situ polymerization method [42], which enhanced the sensitivity of the sensors while maintaining excellent stability and reproducibility. New insights into electrochemical sensor designed and residues detection of profenofos were provided.

N-GQDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal synthesis. This method was simple and requires less equipment, and had good quantum yield. 1.4 g of anhydrous citric acid and 1.2 g of urea were placed in a beaker and 10 mL of ultrapure water was added to form precursor solution. The precursor solution was transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave lined with Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) (100 mL), and then the autoclave was placed in a high-temperature vacuum oven at 160℃ for 5 h.
The processed mixture was cooled and transferred to a 100–500 Da dialysis bag and dialyzed for 24 h. The dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain N-GQDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PANI/N-GQDs composites (in-situ polymerization method)
ce:section.ce:para: 20 mg of N-GQDs powder and 200 μL of aniline were added to a beaker with 20 mL of ultrapure water, and then beaker was placed on magnetic stirrer and stirred in an ice bath for 1.5 h. 10 mL of ammonium persulfate (5 %, w/v) was added dropwise to the mixture. After the reaction was complete, the mixture was transferred to a refrigerator at 4℃ and stirred overnight to allow the material to undergo a complete polymerization reaction

The mixture was centrifuged and washed three times, alternating between ultrapure water and methanol to remove unreacted ammonium persulfate and oligoaniline. Finally, the resulting products were freeze-dried under vacuum to obtain PANI/N-GQDs. The dried PANI/N-GQDs were then ultrasonically dispersed in ultrapure water to prepare a 2 mg/mL solution for subsequent experiments.

Refer to previous method to prepare AuNPs [43]. All glassware was cleaned with aqua regia (>12 h), rinsed with ultrapure water, and dried. For AuNPs synthesis, 50 mL of 0.01 % HAuCl4·3H2O was heated to vigorous boiling (100°C) under continuous magnetic stirring (4000 rpm). After 7–8 min, 1.0 mL of 1 % trisodium citrate was rapidly injected. The solution transitioned from light blue to dark red within minutes, at which point heating was stopped. Stirring continued for 30 min at room temperature (25 ± 2°C) to ensure complete nanoparticle formation. The final AuNPs dispersion was stored in the dark at 4 °C to prevent light-induced degradation. This protocol yielded monodisperse AuNPs with reduced size and enhanced stability.

The screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was a disposable electrode known for its low cost and ease of miniaturization. Before use, the electrode required pre-treatment to prevent the organic solvents and adhesive in the printed carbon paste from affecting its performance. Activation was carried out using cyclic voltammetry at a scan rate of 50 mV/s for 30 cycles, with 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.0) as the electrolyte, and a voltage scan range of −1.5 V to 1.5 V was selected.

Took 5 μL of PANI/N-GQDs solution and added it dropwise to working electrode area on surface of SPCE, and dried it naturally at room temperature. Next, a 5 μL aliquot of the AuNPs solution was dropwise deposited onto the surface of the PANI/NGQDs/SPCE electrode and allowed to dry. Subsequently, 4 μL of aptamer solution was dropwise added to the electrode surface and incubated for 1.5 h to ensure complete formation of Au-S bonds, thereby facilitating aptamer immobilization on the electrode surface. The sequence of aptamer was 5′-(SH)-(CH2)6-AAG CTT TTT TGA CTG CAG GTG AAA AAG AG-3′. Finally, 10 μL of 0.5 % bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution was added to the surface of the Apt/AuNPs/PANI/N-GQDs/SPCE, and non-specific sites were blocked for 30 min. This completed the final assembly of the electrochemical aptasensor, with the assembly process illustrated in Scheme 1 .
Electrochemical differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) curves were performed in 0.01 M PBS (pH 6.5) with a scanning parameter of −0.2 V to 0.6 V in 4 mV potential increments.

Electrochemical cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were performed in a solution containing 0.1 M potassium chloride, 5 mM potassium ferricyanide. The scan rate was 50 mV/s and the scanning potential was −0.2 V to 0.6 V.

In this study, the samples tested included rape, spinach and lettuce, all purchased from Zibo supermarkets. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis confirmed that no profenofos residues were present in any of the samples. The vegetables were initially washed with ultrapure water and left to dry naturally. They were then cut into 1 to 2 mm pieces using a knife. For each vegetable, 2 g of the sample was weighed and placed in a centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of 3 mL of varying concentrations of profenofos. The mixture was thoroughly stirred and left to stand at room temperature for 16 h. Subsequently, 2 mL of acetone and 18 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) were added, and the mixture was allowed to stand for an additional 2 h. The samples were then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected for further analysis.

Fig. 1 A–C presented the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of N-GQDs, which were used to characterize the morphology and size of the quantum dots at different magnifications. The overall morphology of the quantum dots appeared round with a relatively uniform distribution. Using ImageJ statistical software, the particle size distribution of N-GQDs was calculated, revealing that most particles were concentrated within the range of 9–10 nm. The average particle size of N-GQDs was approximately 9.338 nm, following a normal distribution, which indicated that the synthesized N-GQDs exhibited good crystallinity.

Fig. 1D displayed the fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of N-GQDs, which were used to analyze the surface functional groups. Peaks observed at 3380, 2984, 2267, 1639, 1338 and 1104 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of N-H, –OH/C-H, CN, CO, C–N and C–O, respectively. The FT-IR results indicated that N-GQDs were rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, which facilitated effective complexation and dispersion with PANI materials.

Fig. S1 showed the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) plot of N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. S1A, N-GQDs exhibited three peaks at 284.81, 399.8 and 532.64 eV, corresponding to the orbitals of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The relative atomic percentages were 44.31 %, 7.28 % and 48.41 %, indicating that amination occurred during the synthesis of N-GQDs. The XPS spectra of C1s were shown in Fig. S1B, in which the presence of CO, C–O, C–N and C–C/C–H functional groups could be seen. Fig. S1C showed the spectra of O1s, in which the presence of N–O, CO and C–O could be seen. Fig. S1D was spectra of N1s, in which the presence of N-O and C-N functional groups could be seen. From above, it could be seen that N element had been effectively doped onto surface of GQDs.

Fig. 2 showed the electron microscope images of PANI/N-GQDs. Fig. 2A and B displayed the TEM images of PANI/N-GQDs, where PANI clusters were observed to be wrapped around N-GQDs, and the lattice fringes of N-GQDs were clearly visible. This indicated that PANI and N-GQDs were successfully compounded, and both exhibited doped structures. Fig. 2C and D displayed the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of PANI/N-GQDs, revealing that PANI formed an irregularly shaped dendritic structure through an in-situ polymerization reaction. This structure significantly increased the specific surface area of the composite material, which facilitated electron transfer and enhanced the material's conductivity, while also providing more carriers for other materials. The synthesis of PANI/N-GQDs was successful and in accordance with expectation from the combined TEM and SEM images.

The energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) of PANI/N-GQDs was shown in Fig. 3 . In this section, the EDS spectra of C, N, O elements of PANI/N-GQDs were analyzed, electronic and layered images were shown in Fig. 3A and B. Fig. 3C showed total number of EDS spectra, three elements C, N, O accounted for 94.4 %, 3.5 %, 2.1 %, respectively. Because of the N-GQDs, the C element weight percentage of PANI/N-GQDs was significantly higher compared to PANI. Fig. 3D–F showed distribution of C, N, O elements, which were all consistent with overall shape of electronic image, all of which indicated that synthesis of doped structured PANI/N-GQDs nanocomposites was successful.

Fig. 4 showed the XPS plots of PANI/N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4A, the spectral binding energies of PANI/N-GQDs exhibited three peaks at 285.1, 399.98 and 533.1 eV, corresponding to the orbitals of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The relative atomic number percentages of these peaks were 68.94 %, 11.07 % and 19.99 %, which aligned with the changes in atomic number percentages observed in the EDS results. Comparing XPS spectra of PANI/N-GQDs with those of N-GQDs, the positions of characteristic peaks did not change, indicating that composite process did not change structure and properties of quantum dots. Fig. 4B showed XPS spectra of C1s, from which the presence of CO, C–O, C–N and C–C/C–H functional groups could be seen, CO was reduced to C–O and C–N after composite of N-GQDs and PANI. Fig. 4C showed spectra of N1s, from which the presence of N–O, C–N, increase of N–O bond were obvious. Fig. 4D showed energy spectrum analysis of O1s, which showed presence of N–O, CO and C–O functional groups. The presence of N-GQDs in composites was proved by above results. The unidentified peaks in XPS spectra were from substrates used in test samples.
The combined results of TEM, SEM, EDS and XPS could prove that PANI/N-GQDs nanocomposites were successfully prepared.

PAGD was abbreviated from PANI/N-GQDs in the legend of Fig. 5 A and B. The CV curves during the assembly of electrochemical aptasensors were shown in Fig. 5A. After coating PANI/N-GQDs on the bare SPCE electrode surface, the CV peak current was veritably increased, which was attributed to superior electron transfer capability of PANI/N-GQDs. The CV peak current was further increased by dropwise injection of AuNPs onto the surface of the PANI/N-GQDs/SPCE. This enhancement originated from the superior electrical conductivity and catalytic effect of AuNPs, which accelerated the redox reactions of the PANI/N-GQDs. After the introduction of Apt and BSA, the CV peak currents of the sensors exhibited a consecutive and significant decrease. This reduction was attributed to the electrostatic repulsion between the aptamers and the protein nature of BSA, which hindered the electron transfer pathway and reduced the conductivity of the sensors. Upon dropwise addition of pesticide profenofos, the aptamer specifically bound to profenofos molecule produced complex, which further impeded the electron transfer, and the CV peak current decreased dramatically. The stepwise increase in current observed during aptasensor assembly directly correlated with the conductivity enhancement at each stage. This correlation, validated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), confirmed successful layer-by-layer integration of the composite structure.

Fig. 5B showed the EIS variation during aptasensor assembly. The electrodes coated with PANI/N-GQDs showed reduced resistance values compared with bare SPCE. And the impedance values of PANI/NGQDs/AuNPs/SPCE were further reduced, which was attributed to good electrical conductivity of coated materials. The modification of Apt, BSA and profenofos pesticides increased impedance values in order of magnitude. The characterization results of EIS were consistent with those of CV curves, which further indicated that the sensor assembly was successful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Optimization of experimental conditions was essential in sensor design process. By optimizing the experimental conditions, the sensitivity, selectivity and detection efficiency of the sensor could be improved, and the interference could be reduced to meet the needs of different scenarios. The gradient optimization of PANI/N-GQDs concentration, aptamer concentration, pesticide incubation time and pH value of electrolyte in sensor design were carried out as shown in Fig. 6 .

PANI/N-GQDs were signal amplifying elements and carriers of the sensors, and their concentration affected the overall detection sensitivity of sensors. A lower concentration of PANI/N-GQDs was not enough to transfer electrons from the sensors, and a higher concentration will increase the cost of sensor design, so choosing a suitable concentration of PANI/N-GQDs is very necessary. The optimization curve of PANI/N-GQDs concentration is shown in Fig. 6A. It could be observed that the peak current of the DPV of the sensor reached its highest value when the concentration of PANI/N-GQDs was 2 mg/mL, with no significant change in peak current upon further increases in concentration. Therefore, a concentration of 2 mg/mL for PANI/N-GQDs was optimal for sensor efficiency and production cost.

The concentration of the aptamer was one of the most important factors affecting efficiency of sensor. Too low a concentration of aptamers would have insufficient ability to capture the target and decrease the sensitivity, while too high a concentration would drastically reduce conductivity due to electrostatic repulsion between them. Fig. 6B showed the optimization curves for the aptamer concentrations (1 nM, 10 nM, 100 nM, 1 μM, 10 μM), and it could be seen that the DPV peak current of the sensor was the largest at an aptamer concentration of 100 nM. The results showed that 100 nM was optimal aptamer concentration.

Fig. 6C showed the optimization curve of pesticide incubation time. By optimizing the pesticide incubation time, it could ensure that the sensor could shorten the detection time under the premise of binding pesticide molecules sufficiently, and enhanced the detection efficiency. The peak current of sensor was highest at an incubation time of 40 min, when sensor was saturated with aptamer and pesticide binding.

Fig. 6D showed the optimization curve for pH concentration of electrolyte. The conductivity of PANI was higher in acidic environments, but too much acidity affected the activity of aptamer, so it was crucial to optimize the performance of sensor in each pH environment. From the optimization curve, it could be seen that the peak current of sensor was highest at pH 6.5, when conductivity of PANI and aptamer activity reached the best equilibrium value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the pesticide profenofos by aptasensors
ce:section.ce:para: The variation of DPV response signal of electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of different concentrations of profenofos was shown in Fig. 7 A. DPV curves were performed in 0.01 M PBS (pH 6.5). As the pesticide concentration increases, it could be clearly seen that the peak DPV current decreased gradually. When profenofos pesticide existed on the electrode surface of sensor, Apt would be specifically bound to profenofos pesticide, thus adsorbing profenofos pesticide molecules to form a complex on the electrode surface. This reduced the electron transfer ability of sensor, so the peak DPV current decreased with the increase of pesticide concentration. Fig. 7B displayed the fitting curve of the peak DPV current versus the logarithm of profenofos concentration, which exhibited a linear correlation in the range of 1 × 10−1 ng/mL to 1 × 104 ng/mL. Where a negative value on the x-axis represents the value of the logarithm of the concentration of profenofos (1 × 10−1 ng/mL). The fitting curve was described by the equation I = 1.52782–0.08342Lgcprofenofos, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9953, and the calculated limit of detection was 3.59 ng/mL. The aptasensor prepared in this section had a good linear detection range and good detection performance for profenofos pesticide residues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability and reproducibility of aptasensors
ce:section.ce:para: The result of specificity test of aptasensor was shown in Fig. 8 A. The peak currents of 100 ng/mL solutions of malathion, methomyl, azinphos-methyl, chlorpyrifos, profenofos, and a mixture of all pesticides were tested using DPV. The results, as shown in the figure, indicated that the presence of other pesticides (a to d) without profenofos did not reduce the peak current of DPV. However, the single profenofos solution (e) and the pesticide mixture (f) caused a significant decrease in the peak current. This demonstrated the good specificity of the sensor.

Fig. 8B showed the stability test of sensor. At the same time, 15 identical aptasensors were prepared and divided into five groups, which were stored in a refrigerator at 4℃. And the peak current of one group was tested using DPV method at an interval of 7 days each time. From the test results, it was evident that the peak current of the sensors decreased gradually over time. By the 28th day, the peak current had only decreased by 7.679 %, indicating that the sensors could maintain high sensitivity performance for an extended period, demonstrating a long shelf life and excellent stability.

The reproducibility of sensors was shown in Fig. 8C. 5 sensors were set up at same time, 10 ng/mL of profenofos pesticide was added dropwise, and the DPV test was performed respectively, the results were shown in Fig. The standard deviation coefficient of test results of 5 sensors was relative standard deviation (RSD) = 2.21 %, and results of different sensors were similar, which made the test results stable and highly reproducible.

Spinach, oilseed rape and lettuce were selected for spiked recovery testing of aptasensor's ability to detect actual samples. All vegetables were purchased from local supermarkets in Zibo, Shandong Province, and the vegetable samples were uniformly detected by HPLC, and no residues of profenofos were detected. The vegetable samples were pretreated with 1 ng/mL, 10 ng/mL and 100 ng/mL of profenofos, and the aptasensor prepared in this section was used for DPV detection. The test results were shown in Table 1 .

The recoveries of profenofos in vegetable samples by aptasensor ranged from 96.69 % to 101.71 % with RSDs of 1.004 % to 1.994 %. From the test results, it could be seen that aptasensor prepared in this chapter was accurate in spiked recovery test of vegetable samples, with small error and high stability, and the test results proved the application of this sensor in the detection of profenofos residue in vegetables.

In this work, two types of nanostructured materials, N-GQDs and PANI/N-GQDs, were synthesized, combined with advantages and disadvantages of PANI and N-GQDs to compensate each other, and synthesized nanocomposites with excellent performance and good stability. Two materials were characterized using characterization methods to explore electrochemical activity of PANI/N-GQDs. An electrochemical aptasensor was assembled based on PANI/N-GQDs nanocomposites. The sensor showed high sensitivity and low limit of detection for pesticide profenofos. The sensor exhibited good linearity across the range from 1 × 10−1 ng/mL to 1 × 104 ng/mL, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9953. The limit of detection was determined to be 3.59 ng/mL. The sensor designed also showed good specificity, stability and reproducibility, and recoveries of spiked vegetable samples were measured in the range of 96.69 % to 101.71 %. The designed aptasensor provided a more reliable and stable analytical method for detection of profenofos in vegetables, and a new idea for composite and application of nanomaterials.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in supplementary Material.

Yinghui Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Zhenying He: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Mingxuan Fang: Formal analysis. Jingcheng Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jicheng Zhao: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yemin Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 32372438, 31772068), Central Finance Guidance Local Science and Technology Development Fund Project (YDZX2022163), Shandong Province Major Applied Technology Innovation Project (SD2019NJ007), Technological Innovation Guidance Project of Department of Science & Technology of Gansu Province (22CX8NA023), Shandong Province Key R&D Project (2024TSGC0318) and Weifang Science and Technology Development Project (2021ZJ1103).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113572.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
